data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was caused by a synchronised palace debt-austerity spiral triggered by aristocratic consumption outrunning agricultural surplus extraction","mechanism": "Palatial redistributive systems faced compound interest obligations to foreign merchants (Ugaritic and Cypriot) and domestic temple estates, enforced through coercive labor extraction; as marginal lands were brought under plow to service debt, soil exhaustion and peasant flight reduced taxable surplus, forcing further labor intensification and mercenary hiring; this endogenous fiscal overextension made palaces simultaneously unable to pay mercenary garrisons or relieve famine, so armed retainers and subjected populations defected to non-palatial strongmen or migratory bands","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece","Hittite Anatolia","Ugarit","Levantine city-states"],"excludes": ["Egypt","non-palatial Cretan mountain settlements","Syro-Hittite successor kingdoms"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Linear B and Hittite archives showing palace debt levels stable or declining 1250-1200 BC relative to stored surplus","eval": "rubric:std-hist"},{"case": "Evidence of palace grain reserves at capacity through 1190 BC without emergency disbursals or abnormal tax remissions","eval": "rubric:std-hist"},{"case": "Archaeological settlement patterns showing no expansion onto marginal agricultural land in palatial territories 1300-1250 BC","eval": "rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes": "Fiscal-institutional account foregrounding debt politics; Egypt excluded due to temple-estate immunity from palace fiscal extraction and different creditor-debtor structure"}
